### [478] [STEP: Detecting Audio Backdoor Attacks via Stability-based Trigger Exposure Profiling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18103)
*Kun Wang,Meng Chen,Junhao Wang,Yuli Wu,Li Lu,Chong Zhang,Peng Cheng,Jiaheng Zhang,Kui Ren*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces STEP, a new black-box backdoor detection method for deep-learning-based speech models, achieving strong performance metrics in detecting hidden triggers implanted by adversaries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor attacks are a rising threat to deep-learning-based speech systems, with existing defenses either ineffective for audio modalities or relying on unrealistic assumptions.

Method: The proposed method, STEP, profiles test samples using two branches targeting label stability anomalies under semantic-breaking and semantic-preserving perturbations, fusing the results through unsupervised weighting and anomaly detection.

Result: STEP achieves an average AUROC of 97.92% and EER of 4.54% against seven backdoor attacks, demonstrating superior performance across diverse settings, tasks, and physical environments.

Conclusion: STEP is a robust and generalizable solution for detecting backdoor attacks in speech models under challenging black-box, hard-label-only conditions.

Abstract: With the widespread deployment of deep-learning-based speech models in security-critical applications, backdoor attacks have emerged as a serious threat: an adversary who poisons a small fraction of training data can implant a hidden trigger that controls the model's output while preserving normal behavior on clean inputs. Existing inference-time defenses are not well suited to the audio domain, as they either rely on trigger over-robustness assumptions that fail on transformation-based and semantic triggers, or depend on properties specific to image or text modalities. In this paper, we propose STEP (Stability-based Trigger Exposure Profiling), a black-box, retraining-free backdoor detector that operates under hard-label-only access. Its core idea is to exploit a characteristic dual anomaly of backdoor triggers: anomalous label stability under semantic-breaking perturbations, and anomalous label fragility under semantic-preserving perturbations. STEP profiles each test sample with two complementary perturbation branches that target these two properties respectively, scores the resulting stability features with one-class anomaly detectors trained on benign references, and fuses the two scores via unsupervised weighting. Extensive experiments across seven backdoor attacks show that STEP achieves an average AUROC of 97.92% and EER of 4.54%, substantially outperforming state-of-the-art baselines, and generalizes across model architectures, speech tasks, an open-set verification scenario, and over-the-air physical-world settings.

</details>


### [479] [MAED: Mathematical Activation Error Detection for Mitigating Physical Fault Attacks in DNN Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18120)
*Kasra Ahmadi,Saeed Aghapour,Mehran Mozaffari Kermani,Reza Azarderakhsh*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes MAED, an algorithm-level error detection framework, for identifying runtime errors in DNN activation functions due to fault attacks or natural failures in embedded systems. It achieves high detection rates with minimal performance and resource overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural networks in embedded systems are increasingly prone to fault attacks and natural errors during inference, potentially leading to harmful mispredictions. Effective and efficient error detection is essential to ensure the reliability of these systems.

Method: The authors developed the MAED framework that uses mathematical identities to continuously check the correctness of non-linear activation functions at runtime. The approach is tested on ReLU, sigmoid, and tanh activation functions via fault model simulations and implementations on microcontrollers, FPGAs, and in TensorFlow.

Result: MAED showed close to 100% error detection accuracy, mitigated existing fault attacks, and introduced minimal overhead: less than 1% clock cycle overhead on microcontrollers, almost no additional FPGA area demand, and a latency increase of around 20% for sigmoid and tanh.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that MAED effectively detects and mitigates runtime errors in DNN activation functions with negligible resource costs, confirming its potential as a robust solution for enhancing DNN reliability in embedded systems.

Abstract: The inference phase of deep neural networks (DNNs) in embedded systems is increasingly vulnerable to fault attacks and failures, which can result in incorrect predictions. These vulnerabilities can potentially lead to catastrophic consequences, making the development of effective mitigation techniques essential. In this paper, we introduce MAED (Mathematical Activation Error Detection), an algorithm-level error detection framework that exploits mathematical identities to continuously validate the correctness of non-linear activation function computations at runtime. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to integrate algorithm-level error detection techniques to defend against both malicious fault injection attacks and naturally occurring faults in critical DNN components in embedded systems. The evaluation is conducted on three widely adopted activation functions, namely ReLu, sigmoid, and tanh which serve as fundamental building blocks for introducing non-linearity in DNNs and can lead to mispredictions when subjected to natural faults or fault attacks. We assessed the proposed error detection scheme via fault model simulation, achieving close to 100% error detection while mitigating existing fault attacks on DNN inference. Additionally, the overhead introduced by integrating the proposed scheme with the baseline implementation (i.e., without error detection) is validated through implementations on an AMD/Xilinx Artix-7 FPGA and an ATmega328P microcontroller, as well as through integration with TensorFlow. On the microcontroller, the proposed error detection incurs less than 1% clock cycle overhead, while on the FPGA it requires nearly zero additional area, at the cost of approximately a 20% increase in latency for sigmoid and tanh.

</details>


### [480] [Retrieval-Augmented LLMs for Security Incident Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18196)
*Xavier Cadet,Aditya Vikram Singh,Harsh Mamania,Edward Koh,Alex Fitts,Dirk Van Bruggen,Simona Boboila,Peter Chin,Alina Oprea*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The study introduces a system combining RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) with LLMs for cybersecurity incident analysis, achieving high accuracy and cost-effectiveness in detecting attack sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigating cybersecurity incidents is labor-intensive due to the need to analyze large data volumes from various log sources. This paper aims to streamline the process using advanced computational methods.

Method: The researchers employed a RAG-based system that integrates query-based filtering, a query library tied to MITRE ATT&CK techniques, and LLM semantic reasoning. The system was tested across multiple scenarios using five LLM providers.

Result: The system achieved 100% recall in malware traffic analysis, with Claude Sonnet 4 and DeepSeek V3 excelling in performance. In Active Directory attack detection, precision reached 100% with 82% recall. Ablation studies highlighted the necessity of RAG for context.

Conclusion: A RAG-enhanced approach significantly improves the accuracy and cost efficiency of security incident analysis, proving essential for identifying malicious infrastructure and reconstructing attack sequences.

Abstract: Investigating cybersecurity incidents requires collecting and analyzing evidence from multiple log sources, including intrusion detection alerts, network traffic records, and authentication events. This process is labor-intensive: analysts must sift through large volumes of data to identify relevant indicators and piece together what happened. We present a RAG-based system that performs security incident analysis through targeted query-based filtering and LLM semantic reasoning. The system uses a query library with associated MITRE ATT\&CK techniques to extract indicators from raw logs, then retrieves relevant context to answer forensic questions and reconstruct attack sequences. We evaluate the system with five LLM providers on malware traffic incidents and multi-stage Active Directory attacks. We find that LLM models have different performance and tradeoffs, with Claude Sonnet~4 and DeepSeek~V3 achieving 100\% recall across all four malware scenarios, while DeepSeek costs 15$\times$ less (\$0.008 vs.\ \$0.12 per analysis). Attack step detection on Active Directory scenarios reaches 100\% precision and 82\% recall. Ablation studies confirm that a RAG architecture is essential: LLM baselines without RAG-enhanced context correctly identify victim hosts but miss all attack infrastructure including malicious domains and command-and-control servers. These results demonstrate that combining targeted query-based filtering with RAG-based retrieval enables accurate, cost-effective security analysis within LLM context limits.

</details>


### [481] [Toward Reliable, Safe, and Secure LLMs for Scientific Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18235)
*Saket Sanjeev Chaturvedi,Joshua Bergerson,Tanwi Mallick*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper explores vulnerabilities and safety challenges in deploying large language models (LLMs) as autonomous entities in scientific research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to ensure trustworthy deployment of LLMs in science by addressing issues of reliability, safety, and security.

Method: The paper synthesizes a taxonomy of LLM risks in science, proposes a mechanism using multi-agent systems for creating adversarial benchmarks, and suggests a defense framework integrating red-teaming and internal safety agents.

Result: The study outlines methods for defining and evaluating defense strategies for safe and reliable use of LLMs in scientific applications.

Conclusion: A multilayered defense framework is needed for comprehensive safety and reliability in deploying LLM agents for scientific research.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) evolve into autonomous "AI scientists," they promise transformative advances but introduce novel vulnerabilities, from potential "biosafety risks" to "dangerous explosions." Ensuring trustworthy deployment in science requires a new paradigm centered on reliability (ensuring factual accuracy and reproducibility), safety (preventing unintentional physical or biological harm), and security (preventing malicious misuse). Existing general-purpose safety benchmarks are poorly suited for this purpose, suffering from a fundamental domain mismatch, limited threat coverage of science-specific vectors, and benchmark overfitting, which create a critical gap in vulnerability evaluation for scientific applications. This paper examines the unique security and safety landscape of LLM agents in science. We begin by synthesizing a detailed taxonomy of LLM threats contextualized for scientific research, to better understand the unique risks associated with LLMs in science. Next, we conceptualize a mechanism to address the evaluation gap by utilizing dedicated multi-agent systems for the automated generation of domain-specific adversarial security benchmarks. Based on our analysis, we outline how existing safety methods can be brought together and integrated into a conceptual multilayered defense framework designed to combine a red-teaming exercise and external boundary controls with a proactive internal Safety LLM Agent. Together, these conceptual elements provide a necessary structure for defining, evaluating, and creating comprehensive defense strategies for trustworthy LLM agent deployment in scientific disciplines.

</details>


### [482] [PlanTwin: Privacy-Preserving Planning Abstractions for Cloud-Assisted LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18377)
*Guangsheng Yu,Qin Wang,Rui Lang,Shuai Su,Xu Wang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper introduces PlanTwin, an architecture enabling large language models (LLMs) to plan in private environments without exposing raw local context. It employs abstract digital twins to ensure privacy while maintaining planning quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to tackle privacy concerns in cloud-hosted LLMs which need to interact with sensitive local environments (e.g., with source code and credentials) without revealing raw environmental data.

Method: PlanTwin creates privacy-preserving 'digital twins' by abstracting the environment into a sanitized graph and using local gatekeepers for policy enforcement. It employs a formal privacy framework and multi-turn disclosure controls.

Result: PlanTwin maintains full sensitive-item non-disclosure (SND=1.0) and achieves high planning quality (PQS>0.79) with minimal utility loss (<2.2%) across multiple tasks and planners.

Conclusion: PlanTwin successfully bridges the trade-off between maintaining privacy and optimizing planning efficiency, offering a scalable solution for private cloud-assisted planning systems.

Abstract: Cloud-hosted large language models (LLMs) have become the de facto planners in agentic systems, coordinating tools and guiding execution over local environments. In many deployments, however, the environment being planned over is private, containing source code, files, credentials, and metadata that cannot be exposed to the cloud. Existing solutions address adjacent concerns, such as execution isolation, access control, or confidential inference, but they do not control what cloud planners observe during planning: within the permitted scope, \textit{raw environment state is still exposed}.
  We introduce PlanTwin, a privacy-preserving architecture for cloud-assisted planning without exposing raw local context. The key idea is to project the real environment into a \textit{planning-oriented digital twin}: a schema-constrained and de-identified abstract graph that preserves planning-relevant structure while removing reconstructable details. The cloud planner operates solely on this sanitized twin through a bounded capability interface, while a local gatekeeper enforces safety policies and cumulative disclosure budgets. We further formalize the privacy-utility trade-off as a capability granularity problem, define architectural privacy goals using $(k,δ)$-anonymity and $ε$-unlinkability, and mitigate compositional leakage through multi-turn disclosure control.
  We implement PlanTwin as middleware between local agents and cloud planners and evaluate it on 60 agentic tasks across ten domains with four cloud planners. PlanTwin achieves full sensitive-item non-disclosure (SND = 1.0) while maintaining planning quality close to full-context systems: three of four planners achieve PQS $> 0.79$, and the full pipeline incurs less than 2.2\% utility loss.

</details>


### [483] [Cyber-Resilient Digital Twins: Discriminating Attacks for Safe Critical Infrastructure Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18613)
*Mohammadhossein Homaei,Iman Khazrak,Rubén Molano,Andrés Caro,Mar Ávila*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces i-SDT, an intelligent system enhancing Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems (ICPS) defense through predictive modeling, attack detection, and adaptive control, reducing costs and improving operational resilience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ICPS are vulnerable to cyber-attacks that exploit sensor and control loopholes, but current anomaly detection methods cannot efficiently identify attack types and often require costly shutdowns.

Method: The authors propose i-SDT, leveraging Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs) with conservation constraints for robustness, a residual encoder with Maximum Mean Discrepancy for attack type identification, and Model Predictive Control to ensure safe operations without shutdowns.

Result: i-SDT showed significant improvements in detection accuracy, reducing false alarms by 44.1% and operational costs by 56.3% in simulations on SWaT and WADI datasets, with real-time feasible performance.

Conclusion: i-SDT successfully enhances cyber-physical defense through real-time attack detection, classification, and resilience, advancing the operational integrity of ICPS without requiring system downtime.

Abstract: Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems (ICPS) face growing threats from cyber-attacks that exploit sensor and control vulnerabilities. Digital Twin (DT) technology can detect anomalies via predictive modelling, but current methods cannot distinguish attack types and often rely on costly full-system shutdowns. This paper presents i-SDT (intelligent Self-Defending DT), combining hydraulically-regularized predictive modelling, multi-class attack discrimination, and adaptive resilient control. Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs) with differentiable conservation constraints capture nominal dynamics and improve robustness to adversarial manipulations. A recurrent residual encoder with Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) separates normal operation from single- and multi-stage attacks in latent space. When attacks are confirmed, Model Predictive Control (MPC) uses uncertainty-aware DT predictions to keep operations safe without shutdown. Evaluation on SWaT and WADI datasets shows major gains in detection accuracy, 44.1% fewer false alarms, and 56.3% lower operational costs in simulation-in-the-loop evaluation. with sub-second inference latency confirming real-time feasibility on plant-level workstations, i-SDT advances autonomous cyber-physical defense while maintaining operational resilience.

</details>


### [484] [Beyond TVLA: Anderson-Darling Leakage Assessment for Neural Network Side-Channel Leakage Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18647)
*Ján Mikulec,Jakub Breier,Xiaolu Hou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Anderson--Darling Leakage Assessment (ADLA), which leverages the Anderson--Darling test to improve side-channel leakage detection sensitivity compared to traditional Welch's $t$-test-based TVLA, especially under higher-order distributional differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current side-channel leakage detection methods, specifically Welch's $t$-test-based TVLA, are limited in sensitivity when leakage occurs through higher-order differences in distributions, which is critical for evaluating neural network implementations.

Method: A novel leakage detection framework, ADLA, is developed using the Anderson--Darling test to assess the full cumulative distribution functions of traces, without depending on a mean-shift paradigm.

Result: Experiments with a multilayer perceptron (MLP) trained on MNIST and protected using shuffling and random jitter reveal that ADLA achieves improved leakage detection sensitivity, especially for low-trace scenarios compared to TVLA.

Conclusion: ADLA enhances the reliability of detecting side-channel leaks, offering better sensitivity than TVLA in protected implementations, particularly for neural network evaluations.

Abstract: Test Vector Leakage Assessment (TVLA) based on Welch's $t$-test has become a standard tool for detecting side-channel leakage. However, its mean-based nature can limit sensitivity when leakage manifests primarily through higher-order distributional differences. As our experiments show, this property becomes especially crucial when it comes to evaluating neural network implementations. In this work, we propose Anderson--Darling Leakage Assessment (ADLA), a leakage detection framework that applies the two-sample Anderson--Darling test for leakage detection. Unlike TVLA, ADLA tests equality of the full cumulative distribution functions and does not rely on a purely mean-shift model.
  We evaluate ADLA on a multilayer perceptron (MLP) trained on MNIST and implemented on a ChipWhisperer-Husky evaluation platform. We consider protected implementations employing shuffling and random jitter countermeasures. Our results show that ADLA can provide improved leakage-detection sensitivity in protected implementations for a low number of traces compared to TVLA.

</details>


### [485] [Towards Verifiable AI with Lightweight Cryptographic Proofs of Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19025)
*Pranay Anchuri,Matteo Campanelli,Paul Cesaretti,Rosario Gennaro,Tushar M. Jois,Hasan S. Kayman,Tugce Ozdemir*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents a verification framework for cloud-deployed large AI models that uses a sampling-based approach to ensure inference security efficiently, replacing costly cryptographic proofs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the lack of guarantees for correctness and security in cloud-based large AI model inference, and address the infeasibility of rerunning inference and costly cryptographic proofs.

Method: It introduces a protocol using Merkle-tree-based vector commitments paired with statistical sampling of execution traces, creating a balance between efficiency and soundness.

Result: The proposed method reduces proving times from minutes to milliseconds with smaller proofs, validated on ResNet-18 and Llama-2-7B architectures, showing robustness against natural adversarial strategies.

Conclusion: The framework is effective for auditing and large-scale deployments, providing security with reduced computational overhead while supporting rational incentive structures.

Abstract: When large AI models are deployed as cloud-based services, clients have no guarantee that responses are correct or were produced by the intended model. Rerunning inference locally is infeasible for large models, and existing cryptographic proof systems -- while providing strong correctness guarantees -- introduce prohibitive prover overhead (e.g., hundreds of seconds per query for billion-parameter models). We present a verification framework and protocol that replaces full cryptographic proofs with a lightweight, sampling-based approach grounded in statistical properties of neural networks. We formalize the conditions under which trace separation between functionally dissimilar models can be leveraged to argue the security of verifiable inference protocols. The prover commits to the execution trace of inference via Merkle-tree-based vector commitments and opens only a small number of entries along randomly sampled paths from output to input. This yields a protocol that trades soundness for efficiency, a tradeoff well-suited to auditing, large-scale deployment settings where repeated queries amplify detection probability, and scenarios with rationally incentivized provers who face penalties upon detection. Our approach reduces proving times by several orders of magnitude compared to state-of-the-art cryptographic proof systems, going from the order of minutes to the order of milliseconds, with moderately larger proofs. Experiments on ResNet-18 classifiers and Llama-2-7B confirm that common architectures exhibit the statistical properties our protocol requires, and that natural adversarial strategies (gradient-descent reconstruction, inverse transforms, logit swapping) fail to produce traces that evade detection. We additionally present a protocol in the refereed delegation model, where two competing servers enable correct output identification in a logarithmic number of rounds.

</details>


### [486] [ClawTrap: A MITM-Based Red-Teaming Framework for Real-World OpenClaw Security Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18762)
*Haochen Zhao,Shaoyang Cui*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'ClawTrap,' a MITM-based framework for testing the security of autonomous web agents like OpenClaw against real-world network threats, focusing on dynamic attacks rather than static environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in evaluating the security of autonomous web agents under live network threats, as existing benchmarks focus only on static sandbox environments and prompt-level attacks.

Method: The authors develop ClawTrap, a framework enabling customizable MITM attacks such as Static HTML Replacement, Iframe Popup Injection, and Dynamic Content Modification for reproducible and rule-driven security testing.

Result: Empirical studies reveal that weaker models are more prone to trusting tampered observations, while stronger models exhibit better anomaly detection and safer fallback responses.

Conclusion: Real-world security testing for OpenClaw and similar agents should prioritize dynamic MITM conditions over static sandbox protocols to ensure robust evaluation.

Abstract: Autonomous web agents such as \textbf{OpenClaw} are rapidly moving into high-impact real-world workflows, but their security robustness under live network threats remains insufficiently evaluated. Existing benchmarks mainly focus on static sandbox settings and content-level prompt attacks, which leaves a practical gap for network-layer security testing. In this paper, we present \textbf{ClawTrap}, a \textbf{MITM-based red-teaming framework for real-world OpenClaw security evaluation}. ClawTrap supports diverse and customizable attack forms, including \textit{Static HTML Replacement}, \textit{Iframe Popup Injection}, and \textit{Dynamic Content Modification}, and provides a reproducible pipeline for rule-driven interception, transformation, and auditing. This design lays the foundation for future research to construct richer, customizable MITM attacks and to perform systematic security testing across agent frameworks and model backbones. Our empirical study shows clear model stratification: weaker models are more likely to trust tampered observations and produce unsafe outputs, while stronger models demonstrate better anomaly attribution and safer fallback strategies. These findings indicate that reliable OpenClaw security evaluation should explicitly incorporate dynamic real-world MITM conditions rather than relying only on static sandbox protocols.

</details>


### [487] [Functional Subspace Watermarking for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18793)
*Zikang Ding,Junhao Li,Suling Wu,Junchi Yao,Hongbo Liu,Lijie Hu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Functional Subspace Watermarking (FSW), a robust framework for embedding ownership signals in large language models while maintaining high detection accuracy under modifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing model watermarking methods lack robustness, particularly against parameter-level perturbations caused by realistic modifications such as fine-tuning, quantization, or knowledge distillation.

Method: FSW anchors ownership signals in a low-dimensional functional backbone using a generalized eigenvalue approach, adaptive spectral truncation for robustness-utility balance, and vector consistency constraints to preserve semantic performance.

Result: FSW demonstrates superior detection accuracy and robustness compared to state-of-the-art methods across multiple LLM architectures and datasets, even under model attacks.

Conclusion: Functional Subspace Watermarking effectively ensures robust model ownership verification while preserving utility and outperforming existing watermarking techniques.

Abstract: Model watermarking utilizes internal representations to protect the ownership of large language models (LLMs). However, these features inevitably undergo complex distortions during realistic model modifications such as fine-tuning, quantization, or knowledge distillation, making reliable extraction extremely challenging. Despite extensive research on model-side watermarking, existing methods still lack sufficient robustness against parameter-level perturbations. To address this gap, we propose \texttt{\textbf{Functional Subspace Watermarking (FSW)}}, a framework that anchors ownership signals into a low-dimensional functional backbone. Specifically, we first solve a generalized eigenvalue problem to extract a stable functional subspace for watermark injection, while introducing an adaptive spectral truncation strategy to achieve an optimal balance between robustness and model utility. Furthermore, a vector consistency constraint is incorporated to ensure that watermark injection does not compromise the original semantic performance. Extensive experiments across various LLM architectures and datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior detection accuracy and statistical verifiability under multiple model attacks, maintaining robustness that outperforms existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods.

</details>


### [488] [Agent Control Protocol: Admission Control for Agent Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18829)
*Marcelo Fernandez*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Agent Control Protocol (ACP) is a technical framework ensuring controlled governance of autonomous agents in B2B environments, leveraging cryptographic checks for identity, capability, policy compliance, and delegation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a robust governance framework for ensuring secure and controlled operation of autonomous agents within institutional environments.

Method: Introduces cryptographic admission checks for agent actions and formalizes mechanisms like identity verification, capability-based authorization, chained delegation, risk evaluation, policy compliance, and immutable auditing.

Result: The v1.13 specification includes 36 technical documents divided into five conformance levels, a Go reference implementation, conformance test vectors, and OpenAPI specifications for HTTP endpoints. It ensures interoperability, provides verifiable requirements, and prohibits risky behaviors.

Conclusion: ACP adds a strong governance layer for autonomous agents, enhancing RBAC and Zero Trust frameworks without replacing them, enabling secure operations with institutional control.

Abstract: Agent Control Protocol (ACP) is a formal technical specification for governance of autonomous agents in B2B institutional environments. ACP is the admission control layer between agent intent and system state mutation: before any agent action reaches execution, it must pass a cryptographic admission check that validates identity, capability scope, delegation chain, and policy compliance simultaneously.
  ACP defines the mechanisms of cryptographic identity, capability-based authorization, deterministic risk evaluation, verifiable chained delegation, transitive revocation, and immutable auditing that a system must implement for autonomous agents to operate under explicit institutional control. ACP operates as an additional layer on top of RBAC and Zero Trust, without replacing them.
  The v1.13 specification comprises 36 technical documents organized into five conformance levels (L1-L5). It includes a Go reference implementation of 22 packages covering all L1-L4 capabilities, 51 signed conformance test vectors (Ed25519 + SHA-256), and an OpenAPI 3.1.0 specification for all HTTP endpoints. It defines more than 62 verifiable requirements, 12 prohibited behaviors, and the mechanisms for interoperability between institutions.
  Specification and implementation: https://github.com/chelof100/acp-framework-en

</details>


### [489] [Security, privacy, and agentic AI in a regulatory view: From definitions and distinctions to provisions and reflections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18914)
*Shiliang Zhang,Sabita Maharjan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper reviews EU AI regulatory documents (2024-2025), focusing on definitions and provisions for agentic AI, particularly in security and privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the difficulty of crafting precise regulations as AI systems, particularly agentic AI, advance toward greater autonomy, posing challenges in fields like security and privacy.

Method: The paper analyzes 24 EU regulatory documents from 2024-2025, clarifies key definitions, and deconstructs interpretations of security, privacy, and agentic AI compared to related concepts.

Result: The analysis provides a synthesized understanding of the current EU AI regulatory provisions, particularly those addressing security and privacy aspects in the context of agentic AI.

Conclusion: The findings aim to guide policymakers, developers, and researchers on aligning AI regulations with the increasing autonomy and complexity of agentic AI.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies has led to a dynamic regulatory landscape, where legislative frameworks strive to keep pace with technical advancements. As AI paradigms shift towards greater autonomy, specifically in the form of agentic AI, it becomes increasingly challenging to precisely articulate regulatory stipulations. This challenge is even more acute in the domains of security and privacy, where the capabilities of autonomous agents often blur traditional legal and technical boundaries. This paper reviews the evolving European Union (EU) AI regulatory provisions via analyzing 24 relevant documents published between 2024 and 2025. From this review, we provide a clarification of critical definitions. We deconstruct the regulatory interpretations of security, privacy, and agentic AI, distinguishing them from closely related concepts to resolve ambiguity. We synthesize the reviewed documents to articulate the current state of regulatory provisions targeting different types of AI, particularly those related to security and privacy aspects. We analyze and reflect on the existing provisions in the regulatory dimension to better align security and privacy obligations with AI and agentic behaviors. These insights serve to inform policymakers, developers, and researchers on the compliance and AI governance in the society with increasing algorithmic agencies.

</details>


### [490] [Security awareness in LLM agents: the NDAI zone case](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19011)
*Enrico Bottazzi,Pia Park*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The study examines how LLMs respond to evidence regarding their execution environment's security in NDAI-style negotiations, finding reliable detection of threats but inconsistent validation of safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the ability of LLMs to distinguish between secure and insecure execution environments crucial for privacy-preserving protocols like NDAI zones.

Method: A series of NDAI-style negotiation tasks were conducted across 10 language models, examining their responses to different types of evidence about the security of their execution environment.

Result: LLMs universally reduced information disclosure in cases of failing attestation, but their responses to passing attestation were inconsistent, ranging from increased to reduced disclosures depending on the model.

Conclusion: Current LLMs are adept at detecting danger signals but lack reliability in verifying safety. Addressing this through interpretability, fine-tuning, or improved evidence mechanics is critical for effective deployment in privacy-sensitive contexts.

Abstract: NDAI zones let inventor and investor agents negotiate inside a Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) where any disclosed information is deleted if no deal is reached. This makes full IP disclosure the rational strategy for the inventor's agent. Leveraging this infrastructure, however, requires agents to distinguish a secure environment from an insecure one, a capability LLM agents lack natively, since they can rely only on evidence passed through the context window to form awareness of their execution environment. We ask: How do different LLM models weight various forms of evidence when forming awareness of the security of their execution environment? Using an NDAI-style negotiation task across 10 language models and various evidence scenarios, we find a clear asymmetry: a failing attestation universally suppresses disclosure across all models, whereas a passing attestation produces highly heterogeneous responses: some models increase disclosure, others are unaffected, and a few paradoxically reduce it. This reveals that current LLM models can reliably detect danger signals but cannot reliably verify safety, the very capability required for privacy-preserving agentic protocols such as NDAI zones. Bridging this gap, possibly through interpretability analysis, targeted fine-tuning, or improved evidence architectures, remains the central open challenge for deploying agents that calibrate information sharing to actual evidence quality.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [491] [Clinically Meaningful Explainability for NeuroAI: An ethical, technical, and clinical perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18028)
*Laura Schopp,Ambra DImperio,Jalal Etesami,Marcello Ienca*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper emphasizes the need for Clinically Meaningful Explainability (CME) in AI-enabled neurotechnology, advocating actionable clarity over exhaustive technical detail, and introduces a framework, NeuroXplain, to guide design and enhance patient care.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the disconnect between current XAI methods and clinicians' actual needs for explanations in neurotechnology, ensuring ethical, technical, and clinical compatibility.

Method: The authors analyze current XAI methods concerning clinical needs and propose a novel reference architecture called NeuroXplain for designing AI interfaces that prioritize actionable and clear explanations.

Result: The NeuroXplain framework provides actionable technical design recommendations to translate explainability into formats meaningful for clinicians, with potential applications in neurostimulation devices.

Conclusion: Actionable and clinically relevant explanations should replace exhaustive technical detail in neurotechnology interfaces, and the NeuroXplain framework guides this transition to improve AI explainability and ultimately, patient care.

Abstract: While explainable AI (XAI) is often heralded as a means to enhance transparency and trustworthiness in closed-loop neurotechnology for psychiatric and neurological conditions, its real-world prevalence remains low. Moreover, empirical evidence suggests that the type of explanations provided by current XAI methods often fails to align with clinicians' end-user needs. In this viewpoint, we argue that clinically meaningful explainability (CME) is essential for AI-enabled closed-loop medical neurotechnology and must be addressed from an ethical, technical, and clinical perspective. Instead of exhaustive technical detail, clinicians prioritize clinically relevant, actionable explanations, such as clear representations of input-output relationships and feature importance. Full technical transparency, although theoretically desirable, often proves irrelevant or even overwhelming in practice, as it may lead to informational overload. Therefore, we advocate for CME in the neurotechnology domain: prioritizing actionable clarity over technical completeness and designing interface visualizations that intuitively map AI outputs and key features into clinically meaningful formats. To this end, we introduce a reference architecture called NeuroXplain, which translates CME into actionable technical design recommendations for any future neurostimulation device. Our aim is to inform stakeholders working in neurotechnology and regulatory framework development to ensure that explainability fulfills the right needs for the right stakeholders and ultimately leads to better patient treatment and care.

</details>


### [492] [Beyond the Code: A Multi-Modal Assessment Strategy for Fostering Professional Competencies via Introductory Programming Projects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18741)
*Santiago Berrezueta-Guzman,Vanesa Metaj,Stefan Wagner*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper discusses incorporating a multi-dimensional assessment model in a project-based “Fundamentals of Programming” course focusing on game development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To modernize introductory programming curricula by integrating technical and professional skill development.

Method: A Project-Based Learning framework with a multi-faceted assessment model, including real-time demonstrations, screencasts, formal presentations, and peer reviews.

Result: Improved student retention of programming fundamentals, communication skills, and critical thinking.

Conclusion: This pedagogical approach successfully bridges fundamental coding skills with collaboration and professional requirements, offering a scalable model for educators.

Abstract: As the landscape of software engineering evolves, introductory programming courses must go beyond teaching syntax to foster comprehensive technical competencies and professional soft skills. This paper reports on a pedagogical experience in a "Fundamentals of Programming" course that used a Project-Based Learning (PBL) framework to develop a 2D "Maze Runner"-style game. While game development serves as a high-engagement vehicle for mastering core concepts, such as multidimensional arrays, control structures, and logic, the core of this study focuses on implementing a rigorous, multifaceted assessment model structured across four distinct dimensions: (1) an in-situ technical demonstration, evaluating real-time code execution and algorithmic robustness; (2) a technical screencast, requiring students to articulate their work in a concise audiovisual format; (3) a formal presentation to instructors, defending their project's design patterns and problem-solving strategies; and (4) a structured peer-review process, where students evaluated their colleagues' projects.
  Our findings suggest that this multi-dimensional approach not only improves student retention of programming fundamentals but also significantly enhances communication skills and critical thinking. By integrating peer evaluation and multimedia documentation, the course successfully bridges the gap between basic coding and the collaborative requirements of modern software engineering. This paper details the curriculum design, the challenges of implementing diverse assessment pillars, and the measurable impact on student performance and engagement, providing a scalable roadmap for educators looking to modernize introductory computing curricula.

</details>


### [493] [A conceptual framework for ideology beyond the left and right](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18945)
*Kenneth Joseph,Kim Williams,David Lazer*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper introduces a nuanced framework for understanding ideologies as complex socio-cognitive networks. It bridges computational methods and ideology theory for richer analysis of social discourse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches to operationalize ideology in NLP overly simplify it on a left/right axis, disregarding nuanced ideologies on topics like race, climate, and gender.

Method: A conceptual framework is proposed to interpret ideology as a socio-cognitive network, explaining its manifestation in discourse and overlap with social processes like framing.

Result: The framework clarifies connections between tasks like stance detection and natural language inference, while also identifying new research pathways.

Conclusion: The work bridges gaps between computational approaches and ideology theories, offering tools for deeper discourse analysis to benefit both NLP and social science fields.

Abstract: NLP+CSS work has operationalized ideology almost exclusively on a left/right partisan axis. This approach obscures the fact that people hold interpretations of many different complex and more specific ideologies on issues like race, climate, and gender. We introduce a framework that understands ideology as an attributed, multi-level socio-cognitive concept network, and explains how ideology manifests in discourse in relation to other relevant social processes like framing. We demonstrate how this framework can clarifies overlaps between existing NLP tasks (e.g. stance detection and natural language inference) and also how it reveals new research directions. Our work provides a unique and important bridge between computational methods and ideology theory, enabling richer analysis of social discourse in a way that benefits both fields.

</details>


### [494] [Understanding the Relationship Between Firms' AI Technology Innovation and Consumer Complaints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18025)
*Yongchao Martin Ma,Zhongzhun Deng*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This research explores how firms' AI technology innovation influences consumer complaints, showing that AI innovation increases complaints due to heightened threat-related emotions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the underexplored relationship between AI technology innovation by firms and subsequent consumer complaints.

Method: The study uses a multi-method approach, including analysis of S&P 500 panel data (N = 2,758), examination of Reddit user data (N = 2,033,814), and controlled experiments (N = 410 and N = 500).

Result: AI innovation significantly increases consumers' threat-related emotions, leading to more complaints, with AI product innovation causing more complaints than AI process innovation.

Conclusion: Firms investing in AI technology need to consider its psychological effects on consumers and strategize to mitigate complaints, especially with product innovation.

Abstract: In the artificial intelligence (AI) age, firms increasingly invest in AI technology innovation to secure competitive advantages. However, the relationship between firms' AI technology innovation and consumer complaints remains insufficiently explored. Drawing on Protection Motivation Theory (PMT), this paper investigates how firms' AI technology innovation influences consumer complaints. Employing a multimethod approach, Study 1 analyzes panel data from S&P 500 firms (N = 2,758 firm-year observations), Study 2 examines user-generated Reddit data (N = 2,033,814 submissions and comments), and Study 3 involves two controlled experiments (N = 410 and N = 500). The results reveal that firms' AI technology innovation significantly increases consumers' threat-related emotions, heightening their complaints. Furthermore, compared to AI process innovation, AI product innovation leads to higher consumer complaints. This paper advances the understanding of consumers' psychological responses to firms' AI innovation and provides practical implications for managing consumer complaints effectively.

</details>


### [495] [Intellectual Stewardship: Re-adapting Human Minds for Creative Knowledge Work in the Age of AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18117)
*Jianwei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This paper explores how students and teachers can adapt their intellectual responsibilities in AI-augmented learning environments through the framework of intellectual stewardship.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges and potential of generative AI in learning by redefining human intelligence and responsibility alongside AI's expanding role.

Method: The authors propose a conceptual framework based on theories of learning, intelligence, and knowledge creation, emphasizing intellectual stewardship through five guiding principles.

Result: The paper identifies five core principles for intellectual stewardship: being knowledge-wise, intelligence-wise, context-wise, ethics-wise, and focusing on self- and community-growth.

Conclusion: Human adaptation in AI-enhanced educational environments requires wisdom-oriented and socially responsible practices; this framework provides principles for preparing students and teachers to thrive in such contexts.

Abstract: Background: Amid the opportunities and risks introduced by generative AI, learning research needs to envision how human minds and responsibilities should re-adapt as AI continues to augment or automate various tasks.
  Approach: Drawing on theories of learning, intelligence, and knowledge creation, this conceptual paper proposes intellectual stewardship as a human-centered, conceptually grounded framework for advancing creative learning practices with AI.
  Key points: Students and teachers work as responsible governors of intellectual processes distributed across human and artificial systems, guided by five core principles. Being knowledge-wise involves understanding the evolving state of knowledge and taking purposeful actions to advance it. Being intelligence-wise emphasizes making informed choices about how to orchestrate distributed cognitive processes and resources. Being context-wise requires sensitivity to recognize opportunities and risks. Being ethics-wise foregrounds ethical judgment, responsibility, and care in the use of knowledge and intellectual power. Finally, self- and community-growing defines the overarching purpose, aligning intellectual work with personal development and the advancement of collective well-being.
  Contribution: The principles provide a lens for viewing the adaptation of human minds in AI-infused learning environments, calling for the development of meta-level dispositions and capabilities that characterize wisdom-oriented, socially responsible knowledge builders in the AI age.

</details>


### [496] [The Spillover Effects of Peer AI Rinsing on Corporate Green Innovation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18415)
*Li Wenxiu,Wen Zhanjie,Xia Jiechang,Guo Jingqiao*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how companies misrepresent their use of AI (AI washing) and its negative impact, particularly on green innovation, using data from Chinese A-share firms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the growing issue of 'AI washing,' where companies falsely claim AI use to appear innovative, and to understand its impact, particularly on green innovation.

Method: The paper uses large language models for semantic analysis on annual reports of Chinese A-share listed companies from 2006 to 2024.

Result: AI washing significantly hinders green innovation through effects in both product and capital markets, with private firms, SMEs, and competitive sectors being most impacted. Policy simulations suggest actionable strategies to mitigate this.

Conclusion: Proposes targeted government support, differentiated regulatory measures, and an enhanced disclosure mechanism to reduce AI washing's negative effects on green innovation.

Abstract: At a time when the phenomenon of 'AI washing' is quietly spreading, an increasing number of enterprises are using the label of artificial intelligence merely as a cosmetic embellishment in their annual reports, rather than as a genuine engine driving transformation. A test regarding the essence of innovation and the authenticity of information disclosure has arrived. This paper employs large language models to conduct semantic analysis on the text of annual reports from Chinese A-share listed companies from 2006 to 2024, systematically examining the impact of corporate AI washing behaviour on their green innovation. The research reveals that corporate AI washing exerts a significant crowding-out effect on green innovation, with this negative relationship transmitted through dual channels in both product and capital markets. Furthermore, this crowding-out effect exhibits heterogeneity across firms and industries, with private enterprises, small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), and firms in highly competitive sectors suffering more severe negative impacts from AI washing. Simulation results indicate that a combination of policy tools can effectively improve market equilibrium. Based on this, this paper proposes that the government should design targeted support tools to 'enhance market returns and alleviate financing constraints', adopt a differentiated regulatory strategy, and establish a disclosure mechanism combining 'professional identification and reputational sanctions' to curb such peer AI washing behaviour.

</details>


### [497] [The Impact of Corporate AI Washing on Farmers' Digital Financial Behavior Response -- An Analysis from the Perspective of Digital Financial Exclusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18421)
*Li Wenxiu,Wen Zhanjie,Xia Jiechang,Guo Jingqiao*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This paper investigates the phenomenon of "AI washing" by financial technology companies and its negative impact on farmers' digital financial behavior, using data from CHFS2019 and AI investments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the issue of companies overstating their AI capabilities without matching their claims with real investments, which may disrupt consumer trust and behavior in the digital finance sector.

Method: The research constructs an AI washing index using corporate-level data and analyzes its relationship with farmers' digital financial behaviors. It explores mechanisms like knowledge and risk exclusion and examines the moderating role of social capital.

Result: The findings demonstrate that AI washing suppresses farmers' engagement in digital finance, with higher levels leading to reduced behavioral responses. Knowledge and risk exclusions are identified as indirect mechanisms, while social capital mitigates the negative effects of AI washing.

Conclusion: The paper concludes by recommending regulatory improvements, targeted digital financial education, promotion of social capital through mutual aid groups, better consumer protection measures, and initiation of pilot projects for inclusive digital finance.

Abstract: In the context of the rapid development of digital finance, some financial technology companies exhibit the phenomenon of "AI washing," where they overstate their AI capabilities while underinvesting in actual AI resources. This paper constructs a corporate-level AI washing index based on CHFS2019 data and AI investment data from 15-20 financial technology companies, analyzing and testing its impact on farmers' digital financial behavior response. The study finds that AI washing significantly suppresses farmers' digital financial behavior; the higher the degree of AI washing, the lower the response level of farmers' digital financial behavior. Moreover, AI washing indirectly inhibits farmers' behavioral responses by exacerbating knowledge exclusion and risk exclusion. Social capital can positively moderate the negative impact of AI washing; among farmer groups with high social capital, the suppressive effect of AI washing on digital financial behavior is significantly weaker than that among groups with low social capital. In response, this paper suggests that regulatory authorities establish a strict information disclosure system for AI technology, conduct differentiated digital financial education to enhance the identification capabilities of vulnerable groups, promote digital financial mutual aid groups to leverage the protective effects of social capital, improve the consumer protection mechanism for farmers in digital finance, and set up pilot "Digital Inclusive Finance Demonstration Counties," etc.

</details>


### [498] [Student views in AI Ethics and Social Impact](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18827)
*Tudor-Dan Mihoc,Manuela-Andreea Petrescu,Emilia-Loredana Pop*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: A gender-based study analyzed computer science students' views on the societal and ethical implications of AI, showing differential focus and awareness across genders.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand gender-specific views on how AI impacts society ethically and socially, and how this could shape AI education.

Method: A survey was conducted involving 230 second-year computer science students on their perspectives regarding AI’s societal and ethical implications.

Result: Students acknowledged AI’s significant impact on daily life areas like medicine, education, and media. Men focused more on technical domains like autonomous driving and war-related issues, while women highlighted ethical concerns and social media impacts.

Conclusion: Gender perspectives can provide insights into how AI education and awareness can be tailored to address varying societal and ethical considerations.

Abstract: An investigation, from a gender perspective, of how students view the ethical implications and societal effects of artificial intelligence is conducted, examining concepts that could have a big influence on how artificial intelligence may be taught in the future. For this, we conducted a survey on a cohort of 230 second year computer science students to reveal their opinions. The results revealed that AI, from the students' perspective, will significantly impact daily life, particularly in areas such as medicine, education, or media. Men are more aware of potential changes in Computer Science, autonomous driving, image and video processing, and chatbot usage, while women mention more the impact on social media. Both men and women perceive potential threats in the same manner, with men more aware of war, AI controlled drones, terrain recognition, and information war. Women seem to have a stronger tendency towards ethical considerations and helping others.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [499] [A mathematical framework for time-delay reservoir computing analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18706)
*Anh-Tuan Clabaut,Jean Auriol,Islam Boussaada,Guilherme Mazanti*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper introduces a control-theoretic framework for analyzing time-delay-based reservoir computers, providing rigorous definitions and linking them to stability notions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of rigorous mathematical foundations for core properties of reservoir computing, such as separation, robustness, and fading memory.

Method: Utilizes a control-theoretic framework, introducing formal definitions via functional norms and linking them to stability notions like incremental input-to-state stability. Derives explicit bounds through Fourier analysis.

Result: Provides computable criteria for reservoir design and validates the approach on the NARMA10 benchmark and continuous-time system prediction using minimal digital implementation.

Conclusion: The study advances the theoretical understanding of reservoir computing and offers practical tools for design and implementation.

Abstract: Reservoir computing is a well-established approach for processing data with a much lower complexity compared to traditional neural networks. Despite two decades of experimental progress, the core properties of reservoir computing (namely separation, robustness, and fading memory) still lack rigorous mathematical foundations. This paper addresses this gap by providing a control-theoretic framework for the analysis of time-delay-based reservoir computers. We introduce formal definitions of the separation property and fading memory in terms of functional norms, and establish their connection to well-known stability notions for time-delay systems as incremental input-to-state stability. For a class of linear reservoirs, we derive an explicit lower bound for the separation distance via Fourier analysis, offering a computable criterion for reservoir design. Numerical results on the NARMA10 benchmark and continuous-time system prediction validate the approach with a minimal digital implementation.

</details>


### [500] [Fundamental Limits for Sensor-Based Control via the Gibbs Variational Principle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18454)
*Vincent Pacelli,Evangelos A. Theodorou*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new method to derive tighter lower bounds on the performance of feedback controllers using information theory, addressing limitations of existing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to establish more accurate fundamental limits on controller performance as a way to benchmark algorithms, guide sensor selection, and determine task feasibility when feedback is valuable.

Method: A lower bound is derived using the Gibbs variational principle applied to the path measure of states and observations, resulting in a fixed-point equation that is solved via bisection with theoretical guarantees under certain conditions.

Result: The proposed self-consistent bound is shown to perform well on a nonlinear tracking problem, capturing most of the optimal cost across different sensor noise levels, unlike traditional approaches that fail under low-noise conditions.

Conclusion: This approach offers a significant improvement over traditional methods, especially when feedback is crucial, providing tighter and certifiably correct numerical bounds for controller performance.

Abstract: Fundamental limits on the performance of feedback controllers are essential for benchmarking algorithms, guiding sensor selection, and certifying task feasibility -- yet few general-purpose tools exist for computing them. Existing information-theoretic approaches overestimate the information a sensor must provide by evaluating it against the uncontrolled system, producing bounds that degrade precisely when feedback is most valuable. We derive a lower bound on the minimum expected cost of any causal feedback controller under partial observations by applying the Gibbs variational principle to the joint path measure over states and observations. The bound applies to nonlinear, nonholonomic, and hybrid dynamics with unbounded costs and admits a self-consistent refinement: any good controller concentrates the state, which limits the information the sensor can extract, which tightens the bound. The resulting fixed-point equation has a unique solution computable by bisection, and we provide conditions under which the free energy minimization is provably convex, yielding a certifiably correct numerical bound. On a nonlinear Dubins car tracking problem, the self-consistent bound captures most of the optimal cost across sensor noise levels, while the open-loop variant is vacuous at low noise.

</details>


### [501] [Computationally Efficient Density-Driven Optimal Control via Analytical KKT Reduction and Contractive MPC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18503)
*Julian Martinez,Kooktae Lee*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a computationally efficient approach for optimal control in multi-agent systems, transforming a complex cubic scalability problem into a linear one, allowing for improved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the computational inefficiency in achieving desired spatial distributions in multi-agent systems, particularly reducing the cubic scalability of predictive control.

Method: The method involves reducing the high-complexity Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) system for T-horizon planning into a condensed quadratic program (QP) with linear scalability. Additionally, a contractive Lyapunov constraint is integrated to ensure stability.

Result: The proposed approach achieves rapid density coverage with significant computational speed-up, verified through numerical simulations.

Conclusion: This method makes long-horizon predictive control computationally feasible for large-scale multi-agent systems while maintaining system stability.

Abstract: Efficient coordination for collective spatial distribution is a fundamental challenge in multi-agent systems. Prior research on Density-Driven Optimal Control (D2OC) established a framework to match agent trajectories to a desired spatial distribution. However, implementing this as a predictive controller requires solving a large-scale Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) system, whose computational complexity grows cubically with the prediction horizon. To resolve this, we propose an analytical structural reduction that transforms the T-horizon KKT system into a condensed quadratic program (QP). This formulation achieves O(T) linear scalability, significantly reducing the online computational burden compared to conventional O(T^3) approaches. Furthermore, to ensure rigorous convergence in dynamic environments, we incorporate a contractive Lyapunov constraint and prove the Input-to-State Stability (ISS) of the closed-loop system against reference propagation drift. Numerical simulations verify that the proposed method facilitates rapid density coverage with substantial computational speed-up, enabling long-horizon predictive control for large-scale multi-agent swarms.

</details>


### [502] [Using Laplace Transform To Optimize the Hallucination of Generation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18022)
*Cheng Kang,Xinye Chen,Daniel Novak,Xujing Yao*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper uses control theory to study and address the hallucination issue in generation models (GMs). It explores system responses, optimization techniques, and employs Laplace transform analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to mitigate confident errors or hallucinations in generation models by applying principles of control theory.

Method: Generation models are formalized through control theory as stochastic dynamical systems. Simulation of source responses and Laplace transform analyses are applied for improvements.

Result: Training processes of generation models align with system response analysis, facilitating better optimization components to reduce hallucination.

Conclusion: Laplace transform analysis provides a practical framework for addressing hallucination issues in generation models.

Abstract: To explore the feasibility of avoiding the confident error (or hallucination) of generation models (GMs), we formalise the system of GMs as a class of stochastic dynamical systems through the lens of control theory. Numerous factors can be attributed to the hallucination of the learning process of GMs, utilising knowledge of control theory allows us to analyse their system functions and system responses. Due to the high complexity of GMs when using various optimization methods, we cannot figure out their solution of Laplace transform, but from a macroscopic perspective, simulating the source response provides a virtual way to address the hallucination of GMs. We also find that the training progress is consistent with the corresponding system response, which offers us a useful way to develop a better optimization component. Finally, the hallucination problem of GMs is fundamentally optimized by using Laplace transform analysis.

</details>


### [503] [Learning Decision-Sufficient Representations for Linear Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18551)
*Yuhan Ye,Saurabh Amin,Asuman Ozdaglar*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper investigates methods for creating compressed datasets that ensure optimal decision recovery in linear programs with unknown cost vectors. It provides NP-hardness results for exact recovery and introduces relaxed approaches for efficient dataset construction, with guarantees on generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of constructing compressed datasets sufficient for recovering optimal decisions in linear programs, especially given the computational complexity posed by prior methods.

Method: The paper introduces pointwise sufficiency and a polynomial-time cutting-plane algorithm to handle individual cost vectors. It also proposes a cumulative algorithm in data-driven scenarios for stable compression.

Result: The methods achieve high-probability generalization guarantees, improving compression with bounds linked to the intrinsic decision-relevant dimension $d^\star$ instead of the ambient dimension $d$.

Conclusion: Compressed representations can be constructed efficiently under certain conditions, leading to stronger generalization bounds in linear optimization applications.

Abstract: We study how to construct compressed datasets that suffice to recover optimal decisions in linear programs with an unknown cost vector $c$ lying in a prior set $\mathcal{C}$. Recent work by Bennouna et al. provides an exact geometric characterization of sufficient decision datasets (SDDs) via an intrinsic decision-relevant dimension $d^\star$. However, their algorithm for constructing minimum-size SDDs requires solving mixed-integer programs. In this paper, we establish hardness results showing that computing $d^\star$ is NP-hard and deciding whether a dataset is globally sufficient is coNP-hard, thereby resolving a recent open problem posed by Bennouna et al. To address this worst-case intractability, we introduce pointwise sufficiency, a relaxation that requires sufficiency for an individual cost vector. Under nondegeneracy, we provide a polynomial-time cutting-plane algorithm for constructing pointwise-sufficient decision datasets. In a data-driven regime with i.i.d.\ costs, we further propose a cumulative algorithm that aggregates decision-relevant directions across samples, yielding a stable compression scheme of size at most $d^\star$. This leads to a distribution-free PAC guarantee: with high probability over the training sample, the pointwise sufficiency failure probability on a fresh draw is at most $\tilde{O}(d^\star/n)$, and this rate is tight up to logarithmic factors. Finally, we apply decision-sufficient representations to contextual linear optimization, obtaining compressed predictors with generalization bounds scaling as $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{d^\star/n})$ rather than $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{d/n})$, where $d$ is the ambient cost dimension.

</details>


### [504] [Fast and Effective Computation of Generalized Symmetric Matrix Factorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.19147)
*Lei Yang,Han Wan,Min Zhang,Ling Liang*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper develops a method (A-NAUM) for solving generalized symmetric matrix factorization problems, achieving global convergence and efficiency, supported by theoretical guarantees and experimental validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges posed by nonconvex, nonsmooth, and non-Lipschitz matrix factorization tasks often encountered in fields like machine learning and image science.

Method: The authors propose an average-type nonmonotone alternating updating method (A-NAUM) that alternates between updating factor blocks and an auxiliary block. They ensure convergence using line search strategies and leverage theoretical properties like the Kurdyka-Łojasiewicz property.

Result: Numerical experiments validate the theoretical claims, showing A-NAUM's computational efficiency and its ability to reach stationary points in real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The paper advances matrix factorization techniques by introducing a robust algorithm (A-NAUM) with strong theoretical guarantees and demonstrated empirical success.

Abstract: In this paper, we study a nonconvex, nonsmooth, and non-Lipschitz generalized symmetric matrix factorization model that unifies a broad class of matrix factorization formulations arising in machine learning, image science, engineering, and related areas. We first establish two exactness properties. On the modeling side, we prove an exact penalty property showing that, under suitable conditions, the symmetry-inducing quadratic penalty enforces symmetry whenever the penalty parameter is sufficiently large but finite, thereby exactly recovering the associated symmetric formulation. On the algorithmic side, we introduce an auxiliary-variable splitting formulation and establish an exact relaxation relationship that rigorously links stationary points of the original objective function to those of a relaxed potential function. Building on these exactness properties, we propose an average-type nonmonotone alternating updating method (A-NAUM) based on the relaxed potential function. At each iteration, A-NAUM alternately updates the two factor blocks by (approximately) minimizing the potential function, while the auxiliary block is updated in closed form. To ensure the convergence and enhance practical performance, we further incorporate an average-type nonmonotone line search and show that it is well-defined under mild conditions. Moreover, based on the Kurdyka-Łojasiewicz property and its associated exponent, we establish global convergence of the entire sequence to a stationary point and derive convergence rate results. Finally, numerical experiments on real datasets demonstrate the efficiency of A-NAUM.

</details>


<div id='cs.MM'></div>

# cs.MM [[Back]](#toc)

### [505] [EgoAdapt: Enhancing Robustness in Egocentric Interactive Speaker Detection Under Missing Modalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18082)
*Xinyuan Qian,Xinjia Zhu,Alessio Brutti,Dong Liang*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: The paper presents EgoAdapt, an egocentric speaker detection framework for the 'Talking to Me' (TTM) task, excelling under challenging real-world scenarios with missing modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional TTM models struggle with missing visual data, head orientation recognition, and background noise, limiting their applicability in real-world contexts.

Method: EgoAdapt includes three key components: a Visual Speaker Target Recognition module for verbal and non-verbal signals, a Parallel Shared-weight Audio encoder for robust audio extraction in noise, and a Visual Modality Missing Awareness module to handle missing modalities dynamically.

Result: EgoAdapt outperforms state-of-the-art methods on the Ego4D TTM benchmark, achieving a 67.39% mAP and 62.01% accuracy, representing significant improvements.

Conclusion: EgoAdapt proves to be an effective framework for the TTM task, addressing critical limitations of previous approaches and setting a new performance standard.

Abstract: TTM (Talking to Me) task is a pivotal component in understanding human social interactions, aiming to determine who is engaged in conversation with the camera-wearer. Traditional models often face challenges in real-world scenarios due to missing visual data, neglecting the role of head orientation, and background noise. This study addresses these limitations by introducing EgoAdapt, an adaptive framework designed for robust egocentric "Talking to Me" speaker detection under missing modalities. Specifically, EgoAdapt incorporates three key modules: (1) a Visual Speaker Target Recognition (VSTR) module that captures head orientation as a non-verbal cue and lip movement as a verbal cue, allowing a comprehensive interpretation of both verbal and non-verbal signals to address TTM, setting it apart from tasks focused solely on detecting speaking status; (2) a Parallel Shared-weight Audio (PSA) encoder for enhanced audio feature extraction in noisy environments; and (3) a Visual Modality Missing Awareness (VMMA) module that estimates the presence or absence of each modality at each frame to adjust the system response dynamically.Comprehensive evaluations on the TTM benchmark of the Ego4D dataset demonstrate that EgoAdapt achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 67.39% and an Accuracy (Acc) of 62.01%, significantly outperforming the state-of-the-art method by 4.96% in Accuracy and 1.56% in mAP.

</details>


<div id='cs.OS'></div>

# cs.OS [[Back]](#toc)

### [506] [Quine: Realizing LLM Agents as Native POSIX Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18030)
*Hao Ke*

Main category: cs.OS

TL;DR: Quine redefines LLM agents as native POSIX processes, avoiding application-layer orchestrators and relying on OS features for isolation, scheduling, and lifecycle management. It introduces extensions for advanced cognitive workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To eliminate reliance on application-layer orchestrators for LLM agent frameworks and leverage existing OS mechanisms for efficient scheduling and resource management.

Method: Proposed Quine, a runtime architecture mapping LLM agents explicitly to POSIX process semantics, using PID, standard streams, exit status, and recursive execution.

Result: Quine efficiently maps LLM agent functionality onto OS processes, showcasing compatibility with kernel-level controls and highlighting areas where POSIX semantics are insufficient for cognition.

Conclusion: Grounding LLM agents in the POSIX process model provides robustness and OS-native capabilities, but future work should address abstract cognitive runtime needs beyond process semantics.

Abstract: Current LLM agent frameworks often implement isolation, scheduling, and communication at the application layer, even though these mechanisms are already provided by mature operating systems. Instead of introducing another application-layer orchestrator, this paper presents Quine, a runtime architecture and reference implementation that realizes LLM agents as native POSIX processes. The mapping is explicit: identity is PID, interface is standard streams and exit status, state is memory, environment variables, and filesystem, and lifecycle is fork/exec/exit. A single executable implements this model by recursively spawning fresh instances of itself. By grounding the agent abstraction in the OS process model, Quine inherits isolation, composition, and resource control directly from the kernel, while naturally supporting recursive delegation, context renewal via exec, and shell-native composition. The design also exposes where the POSIX process model stops: processes provide a robust substrate for execution, but not a complete runtime model for cognition. In particular, the analysis points toward two immediate extensions beyond process semantics: task-relative worlds and revisable time. A reference implementation of Quine is publicly available on GitHub.

</details>


<div id='physics.chem-ph'></div>

# physics.chem-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [507] [An SO(3)-equivariant reciprocal-space neural potential for long-range interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.18389)
*Linfeng Zhang,Taoyong Cui,Dongzhan Zhou,Lei Bai,Sufei Zhang,Luca Rossi,Mao Su,Wanli Ouyang,Pheng-Ann Heng*

Main category: physics.chem-ph

TL;DR: EquiEwald introduces a machine-learning framework combining SO(3)-equivariance with an Ewald-inspired formulation to accurately capture long-range electrostatic behaviors and improve energy, force accuracy, and data efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing machine-learning interatomic potentials in representing anisotropic, long-range interactions, while ensuring physical consistency and energy-force coherence.

Method: The authors develop EquiEwald, leveraging equivariant message passing in reciprocal space using learned k-space filters and an inverse transform within a SO(3)-equivariant neural network framework.

Result: EquiEwald accurately represents long-range electrostatic interactions, improves energy-force accuracy, demonstrates better data efficiency, and excels in long-range extrapolation for both periodic and aperiodic systems.

Conclusion: EquiEwald establishes a new standard for machine-learning interatomic potentials by providing a physically consistent and accurate representation of long-range interactions in molecular and material systems.
